title: Loneliness and Radicalization
author: Tirkkonen and Tietjen

opening_move.description: Opening from Arendt's analysis of loneliness as a breeding ground for totalitarianism (Origins of Totalitarianism), the paper diagnoses that the political dimension of loneliness has not been incorporated into empirical research on contemporary radicalization, extremism, and terrorism. There is a gap between theoretical debates on loneliness and empirical research on radicalization that this paper bridges.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: An empirically grounded theory of loneliness in contemporary radicalization reveals: for right-wing lone-actor terrorists, fear of individual and collective extinction is central; for Islamist radicalization in Western countries, experiences of discrimination and marginalization are crucial. These two modes require different theoretical and political responses.

rivals_engaged: Arendtian political loneliness theory (extending and correcting)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends Arendt's political loneliness theory by combining it with a critical phenomenological approach that takes subjective experience seriously while situating it in structural social conditions. It applies this combined framework to two case sets: lone-actor right-wing terrorists and female Western ISIS affiliates. The extension of Arendt involves incorporating social exclusion, discrimination, and marginalization as structural conditions shaping the phenomenology of loneliness.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Case study analysis of two sets: (1) lone-actor right-wing terrorists — analysis of manifestos, public statements, investigative psychological assessments of Breivik, Tarrant, and similar cases; (2) female Western ISIS affiliates — qualitative research on recruitment narratives, testimonies, and secondary literature on ISIS foreign fighters. Critical phenomenological reading of case materials.
data_sources: secondary empirical literature on radicalization and terrorism
case study materials on lone-actor terrorists (Breivik, Tarrant)
qualitative research on female ISIS affiliates from Western countries
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Critical phenomenology: combines first-person phenomenological analysis of loneliness with structural-social situatedness. Case analysis guided by Arendtian conceptual framework. Comparative analysis of two types of radicalization.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is an empirically grounded theory paper — closest to the Type 5/6 cluster (empirically anchored normative theory). The empirical evidence is constitutive: the argument about differentiated loneliness types (extinction fear vs. marginalization) requires and is derived from the case analysis. The paper is normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role.

case_type.case_name: Two sets: lone-actor right-wing terrorists; female Western ISIS affiliates

normative_stake.domain: political theory / radicalization / loneliness / terrorism / democracy

confidence_notes: This is one of the most empirically anchored papers in the PSC corpus. The normative-theoretical framing (Arendt, critical phenomenology) is genuine, but the paper bridges philosophy and empirical social science more explicitly than most others. The move_sequence follows the framework-first, case-second pattern (like firinci_orman_2025 and tirkkonen/tietjen is more empirically engaged). The two-case comparative structure (lone-actor vs. ISIS women) is distinctive. Classified normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role.
